coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous stripping determination of cadmium and lead ions based on the N-doped carbon quantum dots-graphene oxide hybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Libo
Liu, Dong
Shi, Aiping
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, a new electrochemical sensor using anodic stripping voltammetry from N-doped carbon quantum dots-graphene oxide hybrid (NCQDs-GO) for the simultaneous determination of cadmium ion (Cd2+) and lead ion (Pb2+) is developed. In the hybrid, both the NCQDs and GO have large electroactive surface areas and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, which supply active sites for the efficient adsorption of heavy metal ions by electrostatic interaction to improve the sensor sensitivity. The NCQDs homogeneously distribute on the surface of GO and play an important role in re-oxidizing metals into ions for stripping analysis. Based on these excellent advantages, the electrochemical Cd2+ and Pb2+ sensor using a NCQDs-GO hybrid modified glassy carbon electrode (NCQDs-GO/GCE) exhibits superior analysis performance than those using just NCQDs on GCE (NCQDs/GCE) and just GO on GCE (GO/GCE), such as wide linear range (11.24–11241μg/L for Cd2+ and 20.72–10360μg/L for Pb2+, respectively), low detection limit (7.45μg/L for Cd2+ and 1.17μg/L for Pb2+, respectively), good reproducibility and selectivity. Moreover, satisfactory testing recoveries are obtained for the simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in lake water and tap water, suggesting that NCQDs-GO hybrid is a promising material for the fabrication of electrochemical heavy metal ion sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Graphene oxide
Cadmium
Lead
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of NCQDs-GO/GCE sensor and electrochemical measurement
Morphologic and structural characterization of NCQDs-GO hybrid
Electrochemical performance of the NCQDs-GO hybrid to Cd2+ and Pb2+
Optimization of conditions for Cd2+ and Pb2+ detection on NCQDs-GO/GCE
Individual and simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+
Determination of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in real water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication process of the NCQDs-GO/GCE sensor.
(A) and (B) TEM and SAED (Inset) of GO and NCQDs-GO hybrid. (C) XRD and (D) Raman spectra of NCQDs, GO and NCQDs-GO hybrid.

(A) Stripping voltammograms of 1μg/mL Cd2+ and Pb2+ in 0.2M pH 5.0 HAc-NaAc at NCQDs, GO and NCQDs-GO hybrid modified electrodes. Conditions: NCQDs, 0.2mg/mL; GO, 1.5mg/mL; deposition potential and time, −1.20V for 3min. (B) FT-IR spectra of NCQDs, GO and NCQDs-GO hybrid. (C) CVs and (D) EIS of NCQDs/GCE, GO/GCE and NCQDs-GO/GCE in 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.5M KCl.
Effect of (A) the concentration of NCQDs, (B) the concentration of GO, (C) pH value of buffer and (D) deposition potential on the stripping peak currents of Cd2+ and Pb2+.

ASV stripping voltammograms for Cd2+ (A) and Pb2+ (B) individual detection under the optimized conditions. Insets are the corresponding calibration curves of the peak currents to the concentration of Cd2+ and Pb2+, respectively. (C) ASV stripping voltammograms for Cd2+ and Pb2+ simultaneous detection. Inset: corresponding calibration curves of peak currents vs. the concentration of analytes. (D) Interference of metal ions (10-fold concentration) on the stripping peak currents of Cd2+ and Pb2+ simultaneous detection under the optimized conditions. The black and red columns represent the I Cd+M/I Cd and I Pb+M/I Pb, respectively. I represents the current, and M represents the selected interferential ions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Simultaneous determination of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in real water.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous stripping determination of cadmium and lead ions based on the N-doped carbon quantum dots-graphene oxide hybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, High-tech Key Laboratory of Agricultural Equipment & Intelligentization of Jiangsu Province, School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology
High-tech Key Laboratory of Agricultural Equipment & Intelligentization of Jiangsu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots loaded graphene oxide hybrid was firstly developed for the simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ by anodic stripping voltammetry.

In the present work, a new electrochemical sensor using anodic stripping voltammetry from N-doped carbon quantum dots-graphene oxide hybrid (NCQDs-GO) for the simultaneous determination of cadmium ion (Cd2+) and lead ion (Pb2+) is developed. In the hybrid, both the NCQDs and GO have large electroactive surface areas and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, which supply active sites for the efficient adsorption of heavy metal ions by electrostatic interaction to improve the sensor sensitivity. The NCQDs homogeneously distribute on the surface of GO and play an important role in re-oxidizing metals into ions for stripping analysis. Based on these excellent advantages, the electrochemical Cd2+ and Pb2+ sensor using a NCQDs-GO hybrid modified glassy carbon electrode (NCQDs-GO/GCE) exhibits superior analysis performance than those using just NCQDs on GCE (NCQDs/GCE) and just GO on GCE (GO/GCE), such as wide linear range (11.24–11241μg/L for Cd2+ and 20.72–10360μg/L for Pb2+, respectively), low detection limit (7.45μg/L for Cd2+ and 1.17μg/L for Pb2+, respectively), good reproducibility and selectivity. Moreover, satisfactory testing recoveries are obtained for the simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in lake water and tap water, suggesting that NCQDs-GO hybrid is a promising material for the fabrication of electrochemical heavy metal ion sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions are environmental pollutants and detecting them has attracted wide consideration, because they are not biodegradable and toxic to humans [1]. Among the heavy metal ions, cadmium ion (Cd2+) and lead ion (Pb2+) act as the most severe risks. For example, the toxicity of Cd2+ mainly comes from its carcinogenicity by impacting the normal gene expression, inhibiting the damage repair of DNA, and inducting the oxidative stress, etc [2]. While Pb2+ possesses detrimental mimicking action, which can occupy the calcium binding sites of some calcium-dependent proteins, resulting in corresponding impairment of physiological functions [3]. Moreover, with the fast development of industry, Cd2+ and Pb2+ are usually widespread and circulatory in soil and groundwater [1]. These metal ions frequently coexist in many environmental samples with potential danger to aquatic life and humans through the food chain. Therefore, developing sensitive and selective analytical methods for precise quantification of Cd2+ and Pb2+ is paramountly required and challenged.

Nowadays, various mature techniques including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [4], optical methods [5–8], mass spectrometry and electrochemical methods [1,9] have been widely developed for the simultaneous analysis of heavy metal ions. Among them, AAS technique is accepted as the standard analysis method for heavy metal ions determination, due to its satisfactory sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy. However, the AAS technique requires relatively expensive instruments and complicated operation process. Thus, development of inexpensive, simple and rapid sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity for simultaneous quantification of trace level of Cd2+ and Pb2+ is urgently needed. For these demands, electrochemical sensors based on some emerging nano- and bio-materials such as inorganic nanomaterials, organic nanomaterials and bio-materials have opened new analytical approach for simultaneous measurement of Cd2+ and Pb2+ [1,10]. For example, Toghill et al. [11] prepared an electrochemical sensor based on antimony nanoparticle modified boron doped diamond (Sb-BDD) electrode, which were used for simultaneous determination of Cd2+ and Pb2+. Metal oxides, such as porous MgO nanoflowers [12] and Fe3O4 nanoparticles [13], have also been widely synthesized to fabricate Cd2+ and Pb2+ electrochemical sensors, which exhibit excellent sensing performance with low detection and wide linear range. In addition, conducting and chelating organic polymers have also been combined with organic nanomaterials to construct sensitive electrochemical sensors for Cd2+ and Pb2+. For instance, inspired by the improved binding ability of poly(2,5-dimercapto-1,3,4-thiadiazole) (PDMcT) to Cd2+ and Pb2+, He et al. [14] developed a sensitive electrochemical sensor by combing PDMcT and multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNTs) to modify glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The limit detection was as low as 10−8 g/L.

Recently, nanoscale carbon-based materials including carbon nanotube (CNTs), carbon nanofiber (CNFs), graphene and fullerenes are generally considered as the ideal candidates electrode materials due to their large effective activity surface area, fast electron transfer rate, good electric conductivity, ect. On the basis, electrochemical Cd2+ and Pb2+ device was fabricated by mixing CNTs and carbon inks on polyester, and then modifying it with Bi and ferricyanide [15]. Such paper-based sensors could detect Cd2+ and Pb2+ in the wide range of 5–150μg/L. In another report, electrospun polyacrylonitrile-based CNFs-modified GCE was coated with Nafion polymer for the simultaneous determination of Cd2+ and Pb2+. In comparison with bare GCE, the sensitivity of CNFs/Nafion/GCE improved markedly due to large active surface area of CNFs [16]. Even so, among all of the carbon-based materials, graphene and its composite [17–19] are most commonly used for Cd2+ and Pb2+ analysis derive from their unique morphology and properties, especially the excellent conductivity.

Nowadays, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel type of luminophor, receive wide attention due to their highly tunable luminescence property, excellent photostability, good solubility and biocompatibility, ease to be functionalized, and so forth [20]. These properties of CQDs, especially the existence of numerous oxygen-containing groups on the surfaces, provide opportunities for heavy metal ions removal [21]. Furthermore, the fluorescence properties also promise various sensing application for heavy metal ions detection [7,22,23]. However, compared with the photoluminescence properties, the electrochemical properties of CQDs are less attractive because of their low conductivity and stacked multilayer structure [20]. To solve these problems, various all-carbon-based composites composed of CQDs decorated on CNTs [24], mesoporous carbon aerogels [25], graphene oxide [26] or reduced graphene oxide [27,28] have been novelty developed for electrocatalytic application. Nevertheless, to the best knowledge, construction of electrochemical sensor based on CQDs for Cd2+ and Pb2+ simultaneous analysis has not been reported.

In the present work, we develop a novel all-carbon-based hybrid composed of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and graphene oxide (GO). NCQDs are uniformly distributed on GO surface and play an important role in re-oxidizing metals to ions for stripping analysis. While GO are served as a novel support to load NCQDs and improve the conductivity and electron transfer rate of the hybrid. Based on the high specific surface area and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups of both NCQDs and GO, electrochemical sensor for Cd2+ and Pb2+ simultaneous detection is fabricated. It is revealed that the proposed sensor exhibits superior analysis performance, indicating its great potential as a promising and convenient material for electrochemical sensors.

Nafion (5wt.%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and urea was provided by Genview. Graphene oxide (GO) was purchased from Nanjing Xianfeng Nano Co. Cadmium and lead standard solution were offered by Aladdin. Citric acid (CA), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6), acetic acid (HAc) and sodium acetate (NaAc) were procured from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). All of the reagents were of analytical grade, and used directly without any purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was operated on a TECNAI F20 field-emission TEM with an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR) were conducted on a VERTEX 70 FTIR spectrophotometer. A D8 ADVANCE (Bruker AXS, Germanny) X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å) was used to record X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on A Thermo ESCALAB 250 instrument with Al Kα radiation (VG Scientific, UK). A Renishaw Raman microscope (model RM2000, 514.5nm) was applied to survey Raman spectrum. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on an AUTOLAB PGSTAT302N workstation with a frequency range from 0.01–105 Hz and using 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with 0.5M KCl as supporting electrolyte.

Firstly, NCQDs were synthesized by one-step microwave-assisted method according to previous report [29], but with a little modification. Briefly, 4.5g urea and 4.5g CA dissolved in 10mL double-distilled water were mixed thoroughly. Next, the transparent solution was reacted in a domestic microwave oven (700W) for 5min. The as-obtained black product was re-dissolved in water and centrifuged at 8000rpm for 20min to remove large carbon residual. And then the supernatant solution was purified via filtered with 0.22μm membrane and dialyzed (MWCO=3500Da) for 48h. Finally, the product was concentrated to obtain black solid powder, which was defined as NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of NCQDs-GO/GCE sensor and electrochemical measurement
ce:section.ce:para: For the fabrication of the sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor, the NCQDs-GO hybrid was achieved by mixing 0.2mg/mL NCQDs and 1.5mg/mL GO firstly. The specific fabrication process was shown in Scheme 1 . Prior to modification, the bare GCE (3mm) was well polished with alumina powder (0.3 and 0.05μm, respectively), and then ultrasonically washed with water, ethanol, acetone and water successively. After placed to dry in air, the treated GCE was coated by 3μL NCQDs-GO hybrid. Finally 2μL 0.5% Nafion film was used as an adhesive to stabilize the NCQDs-GO hybrid and improve the reproducibility of the modified electrode. In addition, as Nafion was cation-exchanging, it would not interfere with the analysis process of positively charged Cd2+ and Pb2+. The finally obtained electrochemical sensing platform was abbreviated as NCQDs-GO/GCE. For comparison, NCQDs/GCE and GO/GCE were prepared via the similar route.

For the analysis of heavy metal ions, anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) was performed on a CHI 832 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Apparatus Inc., China) with conventional three-electrode cell, in which modified GCE was used as the working electrode, a Pt wire and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCL) as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. The analysis procedure consisted of two steps (Scheme 1): First, the NCQDs-GO/GCE was immersed into the HAc-NaAc buffer solution containing metal ions to accumulate and reduce Cd2+ and Pb2+ into metals under the deposition potential of −1.2V and deposition time of 5min. Then the metals were stripped off using ASV in the potential range of −1.2 V∼−0.2V with the step potential of 4mV, the amplitude of 25mV, and the frequency of 15Hz. Finally, the electrode was cleaned at −0.2V for 80s for the next measurement.

Two samples from tap water and lake water (South Lake, Changchun, China) were firstly purified by centrifuging at 10,000rpm for 10min. And then the supernatant were filtered through a 0.22μm membrane. Finally, the samples were diluted with buffer solution for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologic and structural characterization of NCQDs-GO hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of NCQDs, GO and NCQDs-GO were investigated firstly. Fig. 1 A and B illustrate the TEM images of GO and NCQDs-GO hybrid, respectively. As seen from Fig. 1A, individual GO exhibits irregular two-dimensional sheet-like structure with some crimple and fold effect. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) (Inset of Fig. 1A) shows the diffraction ring of GO are blurred, indicating relatively poor crystallization of GO. Upon mixed with NCQDs, the GO sheets are uniformly covered with dense NCQDs (Fig. 1B). While the SAED of NCQDs-GO hybrid (Inset of Fig. 1B) exhibits a ring-like diffraction with some more bright spots. Such an improvement of crystallization should partially be attributed to the introduction of NCQDs [30].

XRD pattern (Fig. 1C) for NCQDs displays a diffraction peak at 2θ=26.9° with the interlayer spacing of 0.33nm, responding to the (002) facet of graphene layers [31]. While the lattice fringe spacing of GO is calculated to be 0.45nm, due to the insertion of oxygen-enriched functional groups and H2O molecule into the layers [32]. However, the interlayer spacing of GO decreased obviously (from 0.45nm to 0.34nm) with the addition of NCQDs. This compact interlayer spacing can be ascribed to the electrostatic interaction between NCQDs and GO. Similar to the previous reports, it exists −NH groups in NCQDs [33], and GO is high presence of oxygen-containing groups, such as −OH and −COOH. Thus the electrostatic interaction between −NH and −OH or −COOH may narrow the interlayer spacing to some extent.

The Raman spectra of NCQDs, GO and NCQDs-GO are presented in Fig. 1D. All the spectra feature two characteristic peaks at 1335 and 1582cm−1 that correspond to D band and G band, accounting for disordered carbonaceous structure and order graphitic structure, respectively. The intensity ratio of the D band and G band (ID/IG) depends on the graphitization or the structural defects of the materials. For the pristine NCQDs, the ID/IG is calculated to be 0.925. The relatively low value is indicative of order graphite structure in NCQDs. Pure GO exhibits that the D band and G band have similar intensity (ID/IG =1.017). The high ratio of surface defects should be attributed to the oxidation of graphite [34,35]. As expected, the ID/IG ratio of NCQDs-GO (0.997) is between that of NCQDs and GO. This might be derived from the excellent graphitization of NCQDs and the abundant structural defects of GO. And the characterization is coincident well with SAED and XRD results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the NCQDs-GO hybrid to Cd2+ and Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 A and Table S1 present the ASV electrochemical performance of NCQDs/GCE, GO/GCE and NCQDs-GO/GCE in 0.2M acetate buffer (pH=5.0) containing 1 ug/mL Cd2+ and Pb2+. As seen, two stripping peaks for Cd2+ (−0.764V) and Pb2+ (−0.540V) are observed at NCQDs/GCE with the current of 3.293 and 4.402μA, respectively (Line a). And 4.590μA for Cd2+ and 3.263μA for Pb2+, are obtained at GO/GCE (Line b). The stripping potentials for Cd2+ and Pb2+ both shift 36mV negatively at GO/GCE compared to the NCQDs/GCE. The potential shift should be owing to the different Coulombic interaction between the analytes and the cation-exchanging Nafion film on the different substrate [16]. Importantly, in comparison with NCQDs/GCE and GO/GCE, hybrid NCQDs-GO modified GCE (NCQDs-GO/GCE) exhibits improved sensitivity for simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ with the current of 11.27 and 9.795μA for Cd2+ and Pb2+, respectively (Line c). The current of Cd2+ at NCQDs-GO/GCE is 3.42- and 2.46-fold higher than that of NCQDs/GCE and GO/GCE, respectively. And the current of Pb2+ at NCQDs-GO/GCE is 2.23- and 3.00-fold higher than that of NCQDs/GCE and GO/GCE, respectively.

In order to seek the contributions to the improved sensitivity of the NCQDs-GO/GCE, FT-IR, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and EIS techniques were conducted to investigate the accounts complementarily. For alone NCQDs, FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2B, line a) reveals the existence of υ(OH) and υ(NH) at the range of 2766–3435cm−1, υ(CO) at 1714cm−1, υ(CC) at 1581cm−1, δ(CH2) at 1350cm−1, υ(CN, CO) at 1170cm−1 and υ(COC) at 1045cm−1 [29,36]. These characteristic functional groups are also confirmed by XPS (Fig. S1). It is valuable to note that various oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, ether or epoxy are consisted in NCQDs. These groups can act as anchor sites to adsorb more Cd2+ and Pb2+ on NCQDs surface [18,19] to improve the analysis sensitivity. However, the electrochemical performance of NCQDs/GCE is still not ideal, and the deeper causes were investigated by CV and EIS using redox species [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as probe. As can be seen from Fig. 2C line a, NCQDs/GCE exhibits low peak currents (12.98μA) and large peak separation (ΔEp, 196mV) for [Fe(CN)6]3−/4, indicating a slow electron transfer of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with the electron transfer rate constant (Κ S) of 0.047s−1 calculated by the Laviron equation [37]. The slow electron transfer rate might be attributed to two aspects: Firstly, the relatively small electroactive surface area of NCQDs (1.5×10−2 cm2) according to the Randles-Sevcik equation:
(1) i=2.99×105
n A C D 1/2
v 1/2
where, i, n, A, C, D, v represent the peak current, the number of electron transfer, the electroactive surface area, the concentration, the diffusion coefficient, and the scan rate, respectively [38]. Secondly, it can be clearly seen from Fig. 2D that the electron-transfer resistance of NCQDs/GCE (Line a) in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 solution is larger than that of either GO/GCE (Line b) or NCQDs-GO/GCE (Line c), suggesting poor conductivity of NCQDs, which may restrict the electron transfer [28].

FT-IR (Fig. 2B, line b) and XPS (Fig. S1) illustrate that GO also has lots of oxygen-containing functional groups like NCQDs, which can complex with Cd2+ and Pb2+ for sensitive analysis. Moreover, as obtained from electrochemical study results, GO has higher surface area of 3.7×10−2 cm2 (Fig. 2C, line b) and lower electron-transfer resistance (1.70 kΩ) (Fig. 2D, line b) in comparison with NCQDs, leading to fast electron transfer (Κ S =0.950s−1) of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at GO/GCE with higher peak current (31.31μA) and smaller ΔEp (75mV) than that of NCQDs/GCE (Fig. 2C). Even so, Fig. 2A reveals that GO/GCE exhibit comparable electrochemical performance for Cd2+ and Pb2+ relative to NCQDs/GCE. The unsatisfactory analysis performance may be result from the fact that although GO sheet can adsorb target metal ions on their surface, it is hard to desorb metal ions from its surface, making pure GO/GCE cannot be renewed conveniently, thus further affect the sensitivity [18].

In the case of NCQDs-GO/GCE, improved analysis properties for Cd2+ and Pb2+ were obtained as shown in Fig. 2A line c. Except that abundant oxygen-containing functional groups distributed in the NCQDs-GO hybrid (Fig. 2B line c and Fig. S1) can increase the adsorbing capacity of Cd2+ and Pb2+, the relatively large electroactive surface area (5.1×10−2 cm2) (Fig. 2C, line c) and fast electron transfer rate (Κ S =0.596s−1) make NCQDs-GO/GCE more suitable for simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+. Comprehensively, NCQDs-GO hybrid not only benefits from large surface area and favorable conductivity of GO, but also from the important role of NCQDs playing in re-oxidizing metals into ions [18]. Therefore, it can be concluded that NCQDs and GO synergistically affect the detection sensitivity of Cd2+ and Pb2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for Cd2+ and Pb2+ detection on NCQDs-GO/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: To get the maximum sensitivity for trace Cd2+ and Pb2+ detection with the NCQDs-GO hybrid modified GCE, some experimental parameters (the concentration of NCQDs and GO, the pH value of buffer, the deposition potential and deposition time) were optimized in 0.2M HAc-NaAc solution containing 1μg/mL Cd2+ and Pb2+.

Fig. 3 A presents the effect of NCQDs concentration on the anodic stripping currents of Cd2+ and Pb2+. As increasing the concentration of NCQDs, the electrochemical response of both Cd2+ and Pb2+ increase gradually from 0.05–0.20mg/mL, and decrease when the concentration exceeds 0.30mg/mL. As discussed above, pure GO or too low ratio of NCQDs to GO is difficult to desorb Cd2+ and Pb2+ from the surface of NCQDs-GO hybrid, leading to poor reproducibility and sensitivity of the NCQDs-GO/GCE. However, an overly high concentration of NCQDs may reduce the electrocatalytic surface area of NCQDs-GO hybrid due to the large aggregation of NCQDs and GO [39]. Thus, 0.20mg/mL NCQDs is selected for the preparation of the electrochemical sensing platform.

The ratio of GO deeply affect the surface area and electron transfer rate. Therefore, the concentration of GO was investigated in the range of 0.2–3.0mg/mL as illustrated in Fig. 3B. Along with the GO ratio increase from 0.2 to 1.5mg/mL, the electroactive surface area and conductivity of the hybrid increase either, resulting in fast electron transfer rate and thus improved peak currents of Cd2+ and Pb2+ with the maximum value obtained at 1.5mg/mL. However, the thickness of hybrid on the surface of GCE increases with too high ratio of GO, which also induces aggregation of GO and restrict the electron transfer rate. To obtain the most sensitive response, the optimized concentration of GO is chosen as 1.5mg/mL.

Influence of the acidity of the accumulation solution on the analysis of Cd2+ and Pb2+ were investigated in the pH range of 3.0–6.0 in 0.2M HAc-NaAc. As shown in Fig. 3C, the peak currents of analytes are small at pH 3.0, and then increase promptly with the maximum value observed at pH 4.0. Afterwards, the electrochemical response decrease notably up to pH 6.0. The increase of peak currents in the range of 3.0–4.0 can be attributed to the electrostatic interaction and complexation mechanism between numerous carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups (negatively charged) of NCQDs-GO and positively charged heavy metal ions. These oxygen-containing groups are similar to those on the surface of graphene or carbon nanotubes, which probably have an appropriate pKa value in the range of 3.0–4.0 [18]. Whereas, the decreased stripping signals at higher pH value may be related to the hydrolysis of heavy metal ions. In consequence, 0.2M pH 4.0 HAc-NaAc is used as accumulation solution.

In stripping measurement, the adequate deposition potential is very critical to achieve the best performance. Therefore, the deposition potential was optimized subsequently. Fig. 3D exhibits that with shifting the deposition potential from −1.10V to −1.30V, the stripping peak currents for both Cd2+ and Pb2+ increase to obtain a maximum current at −1.20V, and then decrease with more negative potential. At the more negative potential exceed −1.20V, H2 will be generated to influence the availability of NCQDs-GO/GCE surface and interfere with the analysis [16]. In order to avoid the evolution of H2 and obtain the maximum peak currents, optimized deposition potential of −1.2V is applied.

The dependence of stripping peak currents for Cd2+ and Pb2+ on the deposition time was depicted by increasing the time from 1min to 7min. As depicted in Fig. S2, the peak currents increase quickly during the deposition time of 1–5min, and then tend to increase slowly until to 7min. Tendency of the change should be owing to the amount of analytes on the NCQDs-GO/GCE surface increase with extension of the accumulation time, thus sensitivity is significantly improved [16,18]. However, the NCQDs-GO/GCE surface will reach to saturation at high Cd2+ and Pb2+ concentration, leading to an insensible increase of the sensitivity and a lowed upper detection limit. Therefore, 5min is used to obtain a high detection sensitivity and wide response range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual and simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, Cd2+ and Pb2+ were detected individually and simultaneously at the NCQDs-GO/GCE in 0.2M pH 4.0 HAc-NaAc buffer by applying an accumulation at −1.20V for 5min. Fig. 4 A displays the typical ASV response toward Cd2+ as increasing its concentration from 5.62 to 5620μg/L. Well-defined and stable stripping peaks are obtained for Cd2+. Inset of Fig. 4A indicates that the peak currents (I p) are linearly proportional to Cd2+ concentration in the range of 5.62–5620μg/L. The obtained linearization equation is I p (μA)=0.027C (μg/L)+0.116, with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998 and the detection limit (3σ method) calculated to be 2.21μg/L (Table S2). For individual analysis of Pb2+, similar stripping peaks are observed at around −0.55V (Fig. 4B), which are linearly increase with the Pb2+ concentration increase from 10.36 to 3315μg/L. The linearization equation is I p (μA)=0.0066C (μg/L)+0.574 (R2 =0.995) with the detection limit calculated to be 0.67μg/L (Table S2). Both the LODs for Cd2+ and Pb2+ are below the guideline value instituted by the World Health Organization (WHO), indicating availability of the developed NCQDs-GO/GCE sensor.

Fig. 4C exhibits the simultaneous stripping analysis of Cd2+ and Pb2+ at the NCQDs-GO/GCE. The recorded ASV curves show individual peaks at around −0.75V for Cd2+ and −0.50V for Pb2+, respectively. The peaks separation is about 250mV, which is large enough to simultaneous and selective detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+. Inset of Fig. 4C presents the linear relationship between the peak currents and the concentration of Cd2+ and Pb2+. As illustrated, the linearization equations are I p (μA)=0.0159C (μg/L)−4.002 (R2 =0.991) and I p (μA)=0.0110C (μg/L)−2.319 (R2 =0.996) for Cd2+ and Pb2+, respectively. And the calculated detection limit (3σ method) for Cd2+ and Pb2+ are 7.45 and 1.17μg/L, respectively. As shown from the slope of regression curve and Table S2, the sensitivities of simultaneous detection slightly decrease in comparison with the corresponding individual detection, which should be attributed to the interaction between Cd2+ and Pb2+ in their co-existence [16]. Even so, the NCQDs-GO/GCE is feasible to detect Cd2+ and Pb2+ simultaneously.

The electrochemical performance for Cd2+ and Pb2+ simultaneous detection at the NCQDs-GO/GCE were compared to some recently reported electrochemical sensors. As listed in Table S3, the proposed NCQDs-GO/GCE displays comparable detection limit, but much wider linear region than that of most reported sensors, which may be due to the synergistic effect of NCQDs and GO.

Selectivity of the NCQDs-GO/GCE towards the determination of Cd2+ and Pb2+ was investigated by adding 10-fold interfere ions including K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ into 0.2M pH 4.0 HAc-NaAc containing Cd2+ and Pb2+. As shown in Fig. 4D, the stripping peak currents for both Cd2+ and Pb2+ change slightly with the errors lower that ±10%, suggesting high anti-interference ability of the NCQDs-GO/GCE for Cd2+ and Pb2+ in co-existence heavy metal ions.

Reproducibility of the proposed NCQDs-GO/GCE was studied by modifying five parallel GCEs with the NCQDs-GO hybrid, and their responses toward the detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+. The obtained relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the five electrodes are 4.78% and 5.62% for Cd2+ and Pb2+ individual detection, respectively. While for the simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+, the RSDs for five electrodes are 4.99% and 4.19%, confirming good reproducibility of the as-fabricated electrochemical sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in real water
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the good performance of the developed method, Cd2+ and Pb2+ in real water including Southlake water (Changchun, China) and tap water were determined by the NCQDs-GO/GCE using a standard addition method. The determination results of the spiked water are summarized in Table 1 . Good recoveries in the range of 90–110% with RSD less than 5% for Cd2+ and Pb2+ simultaneous measurement in real water are observed, indicting the good practical applicability of the proposed method.

In summary, N-doped carbon quantum dots-graphene oxide (NCQDs-GO) hybrid functionalized carbon glass carbon (GCE) is firstly applied for the simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ by anodic stripping voltammetry. Existence of oxygen-containing functional groups supplies abundant anchor sites to adsorb Cd2+ and Pb2+ on the NCQDs-GO surface, and NCQDs play an important role to re-oxidize metal and improve the sensitivity. Moreover, NCQDs-GO hybrid possesses large effective surface area and high electrical conductivity from GO, which is favorable for the fast electron transfer rate. By combining the advantages of both NCQDs and GO, NCQDs-GO hybrid exhibits excellent electrochemical performance for the simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ with wider linear range, good selectivity and excellent reproducibility. Finally it is successfully used to detect Cd2+ and Pb2+ in real water, indicating the NCQDs-GO/GCE is potential for analysis of trace heavy metal ions in practical applications.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475124 and 21675065), the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (2014-37) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160490).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical performance of Cd2+ and Pb2+ at different electrodes (Table S1), comparison of analysis performance of the individual and simultaneous detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ (Table S2), comparison of different electrochemical methods for the detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ (Table S3), XPS spectra of different materials (Fig. S1) and effect of deposition time on the stripping peak currents (Fig. S2) were supplied as Support Information.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.190.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Electrospun carbon nanofiber modified electrodes for stripping voltammetry

SnO2/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite for the simultaneous electrochemical detection of cadmium(II), lead(II), copper(II), and mercury(II): an interesting favorable mutual interference

Development of a rhodamine-rhodanine-based fluorescent mercury sensor and its use to monitor real-time uptake and distribution of inorganic mercury in live zebrafish larvae

Sensitive electrochemical immunoassay of metallothionein-3 based on K3[Fe(CN)6] as a redox-active signal and C-dots/Nafion film for antibody immobilization

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Libo Li obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (CIAC), University of Chinese Academy of Sciences (UCAS) in 2015. Since 2016 to now, she is a lecturer at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests are focused on the preparation of functional carbon-based nanomaterials (including carbon nanodots, carbon nanofiber and grapheme) and their applications in electrochemical analysis and environment.

Dong Liu obtained his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from CIAC, UCAS in 2013. Now he is an associate professor at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research interest is preparation of electrospun carbon nanofibers and their applications in electrocatalysis and energy.

Aiping Shi is a professor of School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering in Jiangsu University. His research interests mainly focus on development and utilization of biomass energy and solar energy, and his research received a number of awards.

Tianyan You received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from CIAC, UCAS in 1999. Since Dec. 2003, she has been working in CIAC as a professor. Currently, she is working in School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University as a full professor. Her research interests are focused on the functional nanomaterials and their applications in precision agriculture.
